ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for breaded deep-fried oysters (kaki fry). FOODON_03302051 (oyster food product) is the closest parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW (Oysters, cooked) is the closest available code; no specific code exists for breaded deep-fried oysters in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for breaded deep-fried oysters as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of kaki fry consists of 4 breaded deep-fried oysters, estimated at approximately 160 g total including panko coating and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Oysters, Eastern, cooked, breaded and fried — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15169)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for Eastern oysters, breaded and fried (NDB 15169 / FDC 175178) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent breaded and fried oysters per 100 g edible portion, which closely matches the preparation method of kaki fry (panko-breaded, deep-fried Pacific oysters). Minor compositional differences between Eastern and Pacific oysters are acknowledged; values are considered a defensible estimate. Sauce contribution is not included in per-100g values as sauce composition is proprietary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kaki fry (breaded deep-fried oysters). GI estimated at 45 based on analogous breaded and fried seafood products (e.g., fried fish fillet, GI ~38–50) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The dominant glycemic contributor is the panko breadcrumb coating; oyster flesh itself contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate. GI of 45 is a defensible mid-range estimate for a panko-breaded fried seafood item. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 25.6 g available carbohydrate per 160 g serving) / 100 = 11.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (417 mg per 100 g; approximately 667 mg per 4-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kaki fry consumption due to its high sodium content from the breading, frying process, and accompanying sauce; a single 4-piece serving may contribute approximately 29% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from panko breadcrumb coating and glycemic load of approximately 11.5 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the carbohydrate contribution from the panko coating (approximately 25.6 g carbohydrate per 4-piece serving) and the moderate glycemic load; portion control and pairing with low-GI vegetables or salad is advisable to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated total fat and saturated fat from deep-frying, partially offset by beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and zinc from oysters
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume kaki fry in moderation given its deep-fried fat content and sodium load; however, oysters are a natural source of omega-3 fatty acids and zinc, which may offer modest cardiovascular benefit when consumed as part of an otherwise heart-healthy diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kaki fry (カキフライ, kaki furai) is a classic Japanese yoshoku dish consisting of fresh oysters coated in wheat flour, beaten egg, and panko breadcrumbs, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. The dish originates from Japan's Western-influenced yoshoku cuisine tradition and is widely served in Japanese restaurants, teishoku sets, and izakayas. In Singapore, it is offered at Japanese specialty restaurants such as The Oyster Bank. A standard serving of 4 pieces weighs approximately 160 g. The exterior is light and crunchy from the panko coating, while the interior oyster flesh is tender, briny, and rich. Kaki fry is typically served with shredded cabbage, lemon wedges, tonkatsu sauce or tartar sauce, and steamed rice. Nutritionally, kaki fry is a moderate-energy food (approximately 199 kcal per 100 g) with notable protein (8.5 g/100 g), moderate fat from deep-frying (11.6 g/100 g), and carbohydrate from the breadcrumb coating (16 g/100 g). Oysters are an exceptional source of zinc (59.2 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (8.75 mcg/100 g), iron (5.1 mg/100 g), and selenium (51 mcg/100 g). The sodium content is elevated (417 mg/100 g) due to the breading and frying process, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 11.5 per 4-piece serving. Clinically, kaki fry is moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its carbohydrate load from panko, high concern for hypertension due to sodium, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat, though oysters' omega-3 content provides partial mitigation.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kaki fry?
Is kaki fry high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy data for Eastern oysters, breaded and fried, which is the closest approved-source match for kaki fry. Pacific oysters (used in Japanese kaki fry) have a slightly different fatty acid and micronutrient profile from Eastern oysters, introducing minor uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate based on comparable breaded fried seafood products from the University of Sydney GI Database. No direct HPB Singapore or Japanese national food composition database entry for kaki fry was available. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for breaded fried oysters. All other -1 values in per_100g are reported from the USDA source. Serving size of 160 g (4 pieces) is estimated based on typical oyster kaki fry portion weights reported in Japanese food composition literature (approximately 35–45 g per piece including coating). Sauce contribution is excluded from per-100g nutrient values as the proprietary special sauce composition is not available. GL per serving = (GI 45 × 25.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 11.5. Available carbohydrate per serving = 16.0 g/100g × 1.6 (serving factor) = 25.6 g. The halal status is marked false as oysters are not universally accepted as halal in all Islamic jurisprudence schools (Shafi'i school permits; Hanafi school does not). Kosher status is false as shellfish are not kosher.

Added 牡蠣フライ as additional Japanese alias.
Sourced nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy FDC 175178 (Eastern oysters, breaded and fried) as closest approved-source match.
Estimated GI at 45 from analogous breaded fried seafood per University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021.
Documented vitamin_d_mcg = -1 sentinel value in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are perfectly consistent.
